title: Credential Compromise via Merchant Category Code Diversification Breakout
narrative: Fraudster uses stolen card credentials to make purchases across merchant categories (MCCs) that deviate sharply from the legitimate cardholder's historical profile. While individual transaction amounts and velocity appear normal, the card is suddenly active at merchant types the owner never patronized. C13 (card merchant-category counter) and C14 (card merchant-ID counter) spike above p90 thresholds, indicating the card is being tested or exploited across retail, digital goods, and service categories in a compressed window. Device fingerprints may be clean or rotated to avoid basic velocity flags. The billing address and email pass verification because the fraudster has full CNP credentials. This pattern exploits merchants with weak fraud controls across diverse MCCs, relying on the fact that each individual authorization looks plausible in isolation.
indicators: C13 > 24 (p90), indicating card has been used at an elevated number of distinct merchant category codes
C14 > 7 (p90), card appearing at more unique merchant IDs than typical cardholder pattern
C11 < 27 (below p90), card-to-address counter remains moderate, suggesting fraudster reuses stolen billing info
card1_distinct_addr1 <= 2, card associated with limited shipping addresses despite merchant diversification
D1 between 1 and 5, device-card pairing count low or moderate, indicating device rotation or new device per session
TransactionAmt between 60 and 200, amounts stay within normal consumer range to avoid amount-based flags
indicator_explanations: Card suddenly appears at merchant types the legitimate owner has never used before
Card is being presented to an unusually high number of different merchants in short succession
Billing address usage pattern remains consistent, fraudster is reusing the stolen cardholder's address
Shipping addresses are limited despite the card being used across many different merchant categories
Device appears unfamiliar or changes frequently, inconsistent with a loyal cardholder's device habits
Purchase amounts fit normal consumer spending levels, designed to stay under merchant review thresholds
summary: Stolen card credentials used across an abnormally diverse set of merchant categories and merchant IDs, while individual transaction behavior remains unremarkable. Fraudster exploits full CNP data to pass basic verification but card-level aggregates expose the breakout from the victim's normal merchant profile.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders exhibit stable merchant-category preferences over time. A sudden spike in C13 and C14 without corresponding behavioral red flags indicates a credential compromise where the fraudster is testing the card's limits across multiple merchant types, often before the cardholder notices and reports the fraud.
